description: Modeling guides for common ExtendSim simulation scenarios. Each guide describes when to use the pattern, which blocks and connections to use, key parameters, and common mistakes.

simple_queue.name: Simple Queue (M/M/1)
simple_queue.description: A single-server queuing system where items arrive randomly, wait in a queue, are processed by one server, and depart. This is the foundational discrete-event pattern for any service or processing system.
useWhen: Modeling a single-server service point (cashier, machine, call agent)
Analyzing waiting times and queue lengths under variable demand
Estimating server utilization and throughput capacity
Baseline model before adding complexity like multiple servers or priorities
Teaching or demonstrating Little's Law and queuing theory concepts

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: Arrivals
blocks.purpose: Generates items (customers, jobs) according to an inter-arrival time distribution

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: WaitingLine
blocks.purpose: Buffers items when the server is busy; tracks queue length and wait time statistics

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Server
blocks.purpose: Processes one item at a time with a configurable service time distribution

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: Departures
blocks.purpose: Removes items from the model and records throughput and cycle time statistics

keyParameters.description: Inter-arrival time distribution. Exponential gives Poisson arrivals (M/M/1 assumption). Mean sets average time between arrivals.
Service time distribution. Mean must be less than mean inter-arrival time to keep utilization below 100%.
Item ranking rule. FIFO (first-in first-out) is standard for M/M/1. Change to attribute-based for priority queues.
Maximum number of items to generate. Set to 0 for unlimited (steady-state runs). Limit for finite-population studies.

keyMetrics: Average queue length (WaitingLine block statistics)
Average wait time in queue (WaitingLine block statistics)
Server utilization percentage (Server block statistics)
Throughput (items per time unit, from Departures block)
Average total cycle time (from Departures block)
commonMistakes: Forgetting to place a Queue before the Activity — without a Queue, items that arrive when the server is busy are destroyed rather than waiting, causing incorrect throughput and lost-item errors

Setting server utilization at or above 100% (mean service time >= mean inter-arrival time) — the queue grows without bound and the model never reaches steady state; always verify utilization = (mean service time / mean inter-arrival time) < 1
Running the simulation for too short a time — M/M/1 systems need a warm-up period before reaching steady state; results collected during transient phase are not representative
Using a constant (fixed) inter-arrival time instead of exponential when modeling Poisson arrivals — constant arrivals prevent realistic queue buildup and underestimate wait times

variations.name: Finite queue (blocking)
variations.change: Set Queue maximum capacity to limit queue length; items that arrive when queue is full are rejected or redirected

variations.name: Multiple servers
variations.change: Replace single Activity with Workstation (maxServers > 1) or add parallel Activities with Select Item Out/In routing

variations.name: Priority service
variations.change: Add a Set block before Queue to assign priority attributes; change Queue rankType to attribute-based ranking

multi_server.name: Multi-Server Queue (M/M/c)
multi_server.description: A queuing system with multiple parallel servers sharing a single waiting line. Items wait in one queue and are routed to the next available server, modeling call centers, bank teller lines, or parallel machines.
useWhen: Modeling multiple identical servers serving one shared waiting line (e.g., bank tellers, checkout lanes)
Finding the optimal number of servers to balance cost against customer wait time
Comparing single-queue/multi-server versus multi-queue/single-server configurations
Evaluating the impact of adding or removing servers on throughput and utilization
Modeling parallel machines or workstations in a manufacturing cell

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: Arrivals
blocks.purpose: Generates arriving items according to inter-arrival distribution

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: SharedQueue
blocks.purpose: Single shared waiting line for all servers; items leave when any server becomes free

blocks.name: Workstation
blocks.label: Servers
blocks.purpose: Multi-server processing block with built-in queue and configurable maxServers parameter; simplest approach for M/M/c

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: Departures
blocks.purpose: Collects completed items and records throughput statistics

keyParameters.description: Number of parallel server channels. This is the 'c' in M/M/c. The Workstation block includes its own internal queue, so no separate Queue block is needed when using Workstation.
Service time distribution per server. Each server independently samples from this distribution.
Inter-arrival time. Total offered load = mean service time / (c * mean inter-arrival time) must be < 1.
Maximum internal queue capacity. Set to 0 for unlimited. When queue is full, new arrivals are blocked.

keyMetrics: Average number waiting in queue (Workstation internal queue statistics)
Average wait time before service begins
Individual server utilization (should be balanced across servers)
Overall throughput versus arrival rate
Probability that all servers are busy (P(delay))
commonMistakes: Using a Workstation block (which has an internal queue) and also placing a separate Queue block before it — this creates two queues in series, inflating wait times and misrepresenting system behavior; use Workstation alone or use separate Queue+Activity blocks
Setting maxServers on a Workstation but forgetting that Workstation service time is per item, not shared across servers — each server independently draws from the service distribution

Connecting parallel Activity blocks without Select Item Out/In routing — items will only flow to the first connected Activity; use Select Item Out (shortest queue mode) to distribute load correctly when using separate Activity blocks instead of Workstation
Not verifying offered load (lambda / (c * mu)) is below 1.0 — with multiple servers the stability condition is different from M/M/1 and is easy to miscalculate

variations.name: Heterogeneous servers
variations.change: Use separate Queue + multiple Activity blocks with different service time distributions; route via Select Item Out in shortest-queue mode

variations.name: Single server baseline
variations.change: Set maxServers=1 on Workstation or use a single Activity to establish the M/M/1 baseline for comparison

variations.name: Priority multi-server
variations.change: Add Set block to assign priorities; configure Workstation or Queue with attribute-based ranking

priority_queue.name: Priority Queue
priority_queue.description: A queuing system where items are served in priority order rather than arrival order. High-priority items jump ahead of lower-priority items in the queue, modeling emergency services, VIP lanes, or job scheduling with urgency levels.
useWhen: Modeling systems where certain customers or jobs must be served before others regardless of arrival order
Evaluating the impact of priority classes on wait time distribution across customer segments
Modeling preemptive or non-preemptive service disciplines in computer or manufacturing systems
Designing triage systems in healthcare, emergency response, or critical systems
Analyzing fairness trade-offs: high-priority items get fast service but low-priority items may wait excessively

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: Arrivals
blocks.purpose: Generates all arriving items; may use multiple Create blocks for different priority classes

blocks.name: Set
blocks.label: AssignPriority
blocks.purpose: Assigns a numeric priority attribute value to each item (e.g., 1=high, 3=low); attribute name must match Queue sort attribute

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: PriorityQueue
blocks.purpose: Ranks waiting items by attribute value; lower attribute value = higher priority when ascending sort is used

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Server
blocks.purpose: Processes items one at a time; always takes the highest-priority item from the Queue

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: Departures
blocks.purpose: Collects items and records statistics; add separate Exit blocks per priority class for segmented analysis

keyParameters.description: The attribute name used for ranking must exactly match the Queue's sort attribute name. Use lower numbers for higher priority with ascending sort.
Set rankType to attribute-based, specify the attribute name, and set ascending=true so items with smallest attribute value (highest priority) are served first.

keyParameters.typical: Multiple Create blocks with different rates per priority class
keyParameters.description: Use one Create block per priority class, each with its own inter-arrival distribution, to control the mix of priority levels.
Service time is typically the same regardless of priority (non-preemptive discipline). Preemptive requires custom logic.

keyMetrics: Average wait time per priority class (use separate Exit blocks or attribute-filtered statistics)
Queue length breakdown by priority level
Server utilization (shared across all priority classes)
Probability low-priority items wait longer than acceptable threshold
Throughput per priority class versus arrival rate
commonMistakes: Setting the Queue sort attribute name that does not exactly match the attribute name assigned by the Set block — the Queue will not recognize the attribute and will default to FIFO, silently ignoring priorities; attribute names are case-sensitive

Using ascending sort with higher numbers meaning higher priority — this sorts backwards; either use lower numbers for higher priority with ascending=true, or use descending=true with higher numbers for higher priority

Using a single Create block and trying to assign priorities probabilistically inside one Set block — it is cleaner and more accurate to use separate Create blocks with different rates for each priority class so arrival rates are independently controlled
Omitting the Set block and trying to assign priority in the Create block itself — Create block does not support attribute assignment; a Set block immediately after Create is required

variations.name: Two priority classes (high/normal)
variations.change: Use two Create blocks with different rates; Set assigns attribute=1 for high, attribute=2 for normal; single Queue with attribute ranking

variations.name: Priority with multiple servers
variations.change: Replace Activity with Workstation (maxServers > 1); Workstation respects Queue priority ranking when pulling items

variations.name: Simple FIFO baseline
variations.change: Change Queue rankType to FIFO to compare priority discipline versus first-come-first-served performance

serial_line.name: Serial Production Line
serial_line.description: A sequential manufacturing line where items pass through multiple processing stages in order, with a queue before each stage to buffer work-in-process. Models assembly lines, pipeline processes, or multi-step production flows.
useWhen: Modeling a manufacturing line with sequential operations (machining, assembly, inspection)
Identifying bottleneck stations that limit overall throughput
Evaluating the effect of buffer sizes between stations on line performance
Analyzing work-in-process inventory levels across the production line
Comparing alternative line configurations (rebalancing cycle times across stations)

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: RawMaterial
blocks.purpose: Generates parts or jobs entering the production line

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: Buffer1
blocks.purpose: Holds work-in-process waiting for Stage 1; size represents physical buffer capacity

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Stage1
blocks.purpose: First processing operation with its own cycle time distribution

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: Buffer2
blocks.purpose: Intermediate buffer between Stage 1 and Stage 2

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Stage2
blocks.purpose: Second processing operation; if slower than Stage 1, becomes a bottleneck

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: Buffer3
blocks.purpose: Intermediate buffer between Stage 2 and Stage 3

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Stage3
blocks.purpose: Third processing operation; final production step before exit

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: FinishedGoods
blocks.purpose: Collects completed parts and records overall throughput and cycle time

keyParameters.typical: Triangular(min, mode, max) or constant per stage
keyParameters.description: Cycle time per stage. The slowest stage (highest mean cycle time) is the bottleneck and determines maximum line throughput.
Physical buffer size between stations. Larger buffers decouple stations and improve throughput but increase WIP inventory cost.

keyParameters.typical: Constant, value = bottleneck cycle time or slightly faster
keyParameters.description: Release rate into the line. For push systems, set faster than bottleneck. For pull/kanban, use Gate blocks to control release.
Use 'fixed' for deterministic lines; 'distribution' adds variability. Variability at any stage reduces effective throughput below theoretical maximum.

keyMetrics: Throughput rate (FinishedGoods items per hour)
Station utilization per stage (identify bottleneck as highest utilization)
Average WIP inventory in each buffer queue
Average total cycle time from raw material to finished goods
Buffer overflow frequency (if finite buffer capacity is set)
commonMistakes: Omitting Queue blocks between Activity blocks — without buffers, a blocked downstream Activity will also block upstream Activities through back-pressure, which may or may not be intended; always explicitly add Queue blocks and set their capacities to match physical reality

Making all stage cycle times equal and expecting perfect throughput — variability at any stage causes blocking and starving even when means are balanced; use realistic distributions (triangular or normal) rather than constants

Setting Create inter-arrival time faster than the bottleneck cycle time — the system never reaches steady state and buffers fill indefinitely; the Create rate should not exceed 1/bottleneck_cycle_time

Forgetting that the Activity block processes one item at a time by default — if a real machine processes batches, use Batch block before the Activity or set appropriate delay to reflect batch cycle time

variations.name: Two-stage line
variations.change: Remove Stage3 and Buffer3 for a simpler two-operation line; useful as a baseline before adding stages

variations.name: With inspection and rework
variations.change: Add a Select Item Out block after one or more Activity stages to route a percentage of items to a rework Activity

variations.name: With shared resources
variations.change: Add Resource Pool and have Activity blocks request resources (operators, tools) that are shared across multiple stages

inspection_rework.name: Inspection and Rework Loop
inspection_rework.description: A quality control pattern where items are inspected after processing and defective items are routed to a rework operation before returning for re-inspection. Models any feedback loop where failures are reprocessed.
useWhen: Modeling a production process with a quality gate and rework loop (non-zero defect rate)
Analyzing the capacity impact of rework on the main production line
Estimating effective throughput when accounting for rework cycles
Evaluating inspection station utilization and rework queue buildup
Modeling any retry or reprocessing loop (loan applications, document reviews, repair processes)

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: Arrivals
blocks.purpose: Generates new items entering the process

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: ProcessQueue
blocks.purpose: Holds items waiting for initial processing

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Processing
blocks.purpose: Main processing operation that items pass through before inspection

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Inspection
blocks.purpose: Quality check operation; all items pass through regardless of outcome

blocks.name: Select Item Out
blocks.label: QCGate
blocks.purpose: Routes items probabilistically: pass percentage exits, fail percentage goes to rework

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: ReworkQueue
blocks.purpose: Holds defective items waiting for rework; may build up if defect rate is high

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Rework
blocks.purpose: Rework or repair operation; items loop back to Inspection after rework

blocks.name: Select Item In
blocks.label: RejoinLine
blocks.purpose: Merges reworked items back into the inspection queue with new arrivals

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: PassedItems
blocks.purpose: Receives items that passed inspection; records final throughput

keyParameters.description: The pass/fail split. Probabilities must sum to 1.0. Output 0 = pass (to Exit), Output 1 = fail (to rework). A 10% defect rate means effective throughput is reduced.
Rework time is typically longer and more variable than normal processing time. High rework time combined with high defect rate can saturate the system.
Inspection time. All items pass through here, so inspection becomes a bottleneck if defect rate is high and rework items re-enter inspection.
Must use probability mode, not condition or attribute mode, for a simple defect rate split. Verify the mode integer value using block_configure or dialog_search.

keyMetrics: Effective throughput at PassedItems exit
Average number of rework cycles per item (average loops before passing)
Rework queue length and average wait time
Inspection station utilization (accounts for both first-pass and reworked items)
Total cycle time including rework (versus first-pass-only cycle time)
commonMistakes: Connecting the rework loop back to the main ProcessQueue instead of to a Select Item In before Inspection — reworked items should return to Inspection, not restart the full process from the beginning; routing to ProcessQueue inflates cycle time and processes items unnecessarily
Setting Select Item Out probabilities that do not sum to 1.0 — ExtendSim may reject items or route them incorrectly; always verify [pass_prob + fail_prob] = 1.0 exactly

Not accounting for the load multiplication effect: with a 20% defect rate and rework always passing on second try, each item consumes 1.2x inspection capacity on average — at high defect rates (>30%), inspection quickly becomes the bottleneck even with short inspection time
Forgetting to add a Select Item In block to merge rework flow back — without it, reworked items cannot re-enter the inspection Activity, causing connection errors or items being destroyed

variations.name: Scrap instead of rework
variations.change: Replace rework loop with a second Exit block (Scrap); items failing inspection exit as scrap rather than being reworked

variations.name: Maximum rework attempts
variations.change: Add a Set block to increment a rework-count attribute; add a Select Item Out after Rework to scrap items exceeding maximum allowed rework cycles

variations.name: In-line with serial production
variations.change: Insert the inspection and rework loop between two stages of a serial line to model in-process quality control

batch_assembly.name: Batch Assembly (Two-Component)
batch_assembly.description: An assembly model where two separate component streams must be joined before the assembly operation. A Batch block holds components until one of each type is available, then combines them for processing. Models kitting, assembly, or any multi-input synchronization.
useWhen: Modeling an assembly operation that requires one item from each of two or more component streams
Synchronizing multiple input streams before a combined processing step
Analyzing the impact of component imbalance on assembly throughput and WIP
Modeling kitting operations where a kit of parts must be assembled before production begins
Any scenario where items from multiple sources must be matched before proceeding

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: ComponentA
blocks.purpose: Generates component A items at the production or arrival rate for stream A

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: ComponentB
blocks.purpose: Generates component B items at the production or arrival rate for stream B

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: BufferA
blocks.purpose: Holds component A items waiting to be matched with a component B item for batching

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: BufferB
blocks.purpose: Holds component B items waiting to be matched with a component A item for batching

blocks.name: Batch
blocks.label: Assembler
blocks.purpose: Collects one item from each connected input Queue and combines them into a single batched item for downstream processing

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: AssemblyQueue
blocks.purpose: Holds batched items waiting for the assembly Activity

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Assembly
blocks.purpose: Performs the assembly operation on the combined batch item

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: FinishedProduct
blocks.purpose: Collects completed assemblies and records throughput

keyParameters.typical: batchSize=1 per input (permanent batch of N=2), batchType=permanent
keyParameters.description: For assembly, use permanent batching (components are permanently combined). batchSize typically 1 per input connector. Temporary batching groups items that will be unbatched later.
Component A arrival rate. If rate differs significantly from component B, the slower stream becomes the bottleneck and the faster stream builds up in its buffer.
Component B arrival rate. Ideally balanced with component A rate. Imbalance causes one buffer to grow without bound.
If components must be matched by a specific attribute (e.g., serial number or order ID), set matchAttribute. Without matching, Batch combines any available item from each input.

keyMetrics: Assembly throughput (limited by slower component stream)
Buffer length for each component stream (imbalance indicator)
Batch wait time (how long until matching component arrives)
Assembly Activity utilization
WIP ratio between BufferA and BufferB (large ratio indicates flow imbalance)
commonMistakes: Connecting component Create blocks directly to the Batch block without Queue buffers — the Batch block needs Queue blocks on each input because it pulls from Queue outputs, not directly from Create outputs; without Queues, components cannot accumulate and Batch will stall

Setting mismatched component arrival rates and not accounting for the resulting buffer imbalance — if component A arrives twice as fast as component B, the BufferA will grow indefinitely while BufferB stays near empty; always balance mean arrival rates across all component streams

Using temporary batch mode when permanent assembly is intended — temporary batching allows items to be unbatched later (via Unbatch block), which is wrong for assembly; use permanent batch mode when components are physically combined

Expecting Batch block throughput to exceed the rate of the slowest component stream — the assembly rate is limited by min(rateA, rateB); adding more Assembly Activities downstream cannot compensate for a slow component supplier

variations.name: Three-component assembly
variations.change: Add a third Create+Queue pair; connect third Queue to Batch third input. Batch waits for one item from each of three inputs before releasing

variations.name: Variable batch size
variations.change: Collect N identical items before processing (e.g., packaging): single Create→Queue→Batch with batchSize=N from one input

variations.name: With serial line downstream
variations.change: Route assembled items through multiple processing stages after the Batch+Assembly step for post-assembly operations

supply_chain.name: Multi-Stage Supply Chain
supply_chain.description: A sequential supply chain model with warehouse, transportation, and retail stages. Each stage has its own processing time and queue, representing the flow of goods from supplier through distribution to end customer.
useWhen: Modeling end-to-end supply chain flows from supplier to customer across multiple stages
Analyzing lead times and inventory levels at each stage of the supply chain
Identifying bottleneck stages that constrain delivery performance
Evaluating the bullwhip effect and demand variability amplification upstream
Comparing supply chain configurations (number of distribution centers, transport modes)

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: CustomerOrders
blocks.purpose: Generates customer orders or demand events according to demand distribution

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: WarehouseQueue
blocks.purpose: Order backlog at warehouse stage; represents orders waiting to be picked and packed

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Warehouse
blocks.purpose: Warehouse processing: picking, packing, and staging for shipment

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: TransportQueue
blocks.purpose: Shipments waiting for transport (staging area); represents consolidation or loading time

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Transport
blocks.purpose: Transportation leg from warehouse to distribution center or retail; models transit time

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: RetailQueue
blocks.purpose: Inbound goods waiting to be received and processed at retail or delivery point

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Retail
blocks.purpose: Final delivery or retail processing step; represents last-mile or store receiving

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: FulfilledOrders
blocks.purpose: Records fulfilled orders; cycle time from Create to Exit represents total order lead time

keyParameters.description: Transit time between supply chain stages. This is often the largest component of total lead time and is typically modeled as a constant or slightly variable duration.
Order processing time at warehouse. Highly variable processing times cause queue buildup and increase order lead time variability.

keyParameters.typical: Poisson arrivals (exponential inter-arrival) or scheduled demand
keyParameters.description: Customer order arrival pattern. Seasonal or bursty demand causes temporary queue buildup at each stage.
Receiving and processing time at final destination. Often much shorter than transport time but still contributes to total lead time.

keyMetrics: Total order lead time from placement to fulfillment (Create to Exit cycle time)
Stage-by-stage queue lengths (indicator of bottleneck location)
Utilization at each processing stage
On-time delivery rate (fraction of orders fulfilled within target lead time)
WIP inventory value at each stage (queue length * average item value)
commonMistakes: Modeling supply chain stages with constant (zero-variability) processing times — real supply chains have significant variability in picking, transport, and receiving times; constant times underestimate queue buildup and lead time variability

Not including Queue blocks between stages — items would flow straight through without buffering, preventing the model from capturing queuing and delay effects that are the primary source of supply chain variability

Treating the model as a single flow without modeling stockouts or capacity limits — if any stage has finite capacity (e.g., truck capacity), add Queue maximum capacity limits and handle overflow items explicitly

Using the same time units for all stages without conversion — warehouse operations may be in hours while transport is in days; ensure simulation time units are consistent or use time_convert tool to align

variations.name: With inventory control (reorder point)
variations.change: Add Gate blocks at each stage to hold orders when inventory is below reorder point; model replenishment as a separate item stream

variations.name: With shared transport resources
variations.change: Add Resource Pool for trucks or transport capacity; Transport Activity requests from pool, limiting how many shipments can be in transit simultaneously

variations.name: With shift-based warehouse operations
variations.change: Add Shift block to Warehouse Activity to restrict processing to working hours; queue accumulates during off-hours

inventory_management.name: Inventory Management (Reorder Point)
inventory_management.description: A reorder-point inventory model where a Gate block checks stock level and either fulfills demand immediately from stock or places a replenishment order. Models the classic (s, S) or (R, Q) inventory policy.
useWhen: Modeling an inventory system with a defined reorder point and order quantity policy
Analyzing service level (fill rate) and stockout frequency under variable demand
Evaluating the trade-off between safety stock levels and holding costs
Modeling any threshold-triggered replenishment system (kanban, min-max, reorder point)
Determining optimal reorder point and order quantity for a given service level target

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: DemandOrders
blocks.purpose: Generates customer demand events (orders); each item represents one demand unit

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: OrderBacklog
blocks.purpose: Holds demand orders that cannot be immediately fulfilled due to stockout; represents backorders

blocks.name: Gate
blocks.label: StockCheck
blocks.purpose: Opens when stock is available; closes when stock is depleted below reorder point, triggering replenishment

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: Fulfill
blocks.purpose: Order fulfillment processing time (picking, packing); represents the time to fulfill from available stock

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: Replenishment
blocks.purpose: Generates replenishment arrivals after lead time; represents supplier delivery

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: ReplenishLead
blocks.purpose: Models replenishment lead time from order placement to stock arrival

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: FulfilledDemand
blocks.purpose: Records fulfilled orders; throughput versus DemandOrders rate gives fill rate

keyParameters.typical: initialState=open (stock available), controlled by inventory level signal
keyParameters.description: Gate opens when stock is available and closes on stockout. In practice, link a Value connector from an inventory counter to the Gate to implement the reorder-point check.

keyParameters.typical: Triggered by stockout event, not time-based
keyParameters.description: Replenishment orders are triggered when stock hits the reorder point. Model as scheduled or use an Equation block to trigger replenishment based on inventory level.
Supplier lead time from order placement to stock receipt. Longer and more variable lead times require higher safety stock to maintain the same service level.
Order fulfillment processing time. This is typically much shorter than replenishment lead time and contributes minimally to stockout duration.

keyMetrics: Fill rate: fraction of orders fulfilled without backorder (FulfilledDemand throughput / DemandOrders throughput)
Average backorder level (OrderBacklog queue length over time)
Average stockout duration per cycle
Average on-hand inventory level (between replenishment cycles)
Replenishment frequency (number of orders placed per time period)
commonMistakes: Modeling inventory as item count without a Gate — without a Gate to block demand when stock is zero, items flow through freely regardless of stock level, making stockouts invisible in the model

Using a single Create block for both demand and replenishment instead of modeling the reorder trigger explicitly — replenishment should only occur when stock falls below the reorder point, not on a fixed schedule independent of demand

Not modeling backorders separately from lost sales — if the model routes unfulfilled demand to Exit rather than OrderBacklog Queue, it is modeling lost sales (not backorders); choose the correct stockout behavior for your scenario
Ignoring the variability in replenishment lead time — using a constant lead time when real lead times are variable underestimates required safety stock and overestimates achievable service levels

variations.name: Lost sales (no backorder)
variations.change: Remove OrderBacklog Queue; add a second Exit for LostSales; route excess demand directly to LostSales when Gate is closed

variations.name: Periodic review (R, S) policy
variations.change: Replace continuous Gate monitoring with a scheduled Create that triggers inventory review at fixed intervals; order up to S when inventory falls below s

variations.name: Multi-echelon supply chain
variations.change: Chain multiple inventory stages (retail, distribution center, supplier) with each stage having its own reorder logic and lead time

shared_resources.name: Shared Resources
shared_resources.description: Multiple processes compete for a limited pool of shared resources (operators, tools, vehicles). Each process requests resources when needed, uses them during processing, and releases them for other processes to use.
useWhen: Modeling operators, technicians, or vehicles shared across multiple production lines or service points
Analyzing resource contention and its impact on throughput and waiting time
Determining the minimum number of shared resources to achieve a target utilization or wait time
Modeling tool cribs, forklifts, or test equipment shared by multiple work centers
Any scenario where a finite pool of resources is the primary constraint on system throughput

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: ArrivalsA
blocks.purpose: Generates items for process A that will compete for shared resources

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: ArrivalsB
blocks.purpose: Generates items for process B that will also compete for the same shared resource pool

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: QueueA
blocks.purpose: Holds process A items waiting for resource availability; wait time reflects resource contention

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: QueueB
blocks.purpose: Holds process B items waiting for resource availability from the same pool

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: ProcessA
blocks.purpose: Process A activity; requests and holds resources from the Resource Pool during processing

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: ProcessB
blocks.purpose: Process B activity; competes with Process A for resources from the same Resource Pool

blocks.name: Resource Pool
blocks.label: SharedPool
blocks.purpose: Defines the total number of available shared resources; Activity blocks request from and release to this pool

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: CompletedA
blocks.purpose: Collects completed process A items

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: CompletedB
blocks.purpose: Collects completed process B items

keyParameters.description: Total number of available shared resources. This is the key decision variable. Too few causes excessive waiting; too many wastes capacity.
Each Queue must be linked to the Resource Pool. Items wait in queue until both a downstream Activity is free AND the required number of resources are available.
Resource holding time. Resources are held for the entire activity duration, so longer service times mean resources are unavailable to other processes for longer.
Rule for allocating resources when multiple queues compete. FIFO allocates to the longest-waiting request. Priority allocation favors higher-priority processes.

keyMetrics: Resource pool utilization (average fraction of resources in use)
Average wait time in each queue due to resource unavailability
Average queue length for each process (resource contention indicator)
Throughput of each process versus arrival rate
Resource allocation fairness across competing processes
commonMistakes: Forgetting to link the Queue block to the Resource Pool — in ExtendSim, the Queue block (not the Activity block) requests resources; if the Queue's resourcePoolBlockId is not set, resources are never requested and items flow through without resource constraint

Connecting the Resource Pool directly to the Activity block instead of to the Queue block — resources must be requested at the Queue so items wait for both server availability AND resource availability; resource request at Activity happens too late
Setting resourcesNeeded to a number larger than initialResources — if any process needs more resources than exist in the pool, those items will wait forever and the simulation will deadlock

Not including a Queue block before each Activity when using Resource Pool — without a Queue, the resource request linkage cannot be established and resources will not be properly requested or released

variations.name: Priority resource allocation
variations.change: Set Queue allocationRule to priority-based and assign priority attributes to items; high-priority items get resources before low-priority items when pool is contended

variations.name: Multiple resource types
variations.change: Add a second Resource Pool for a different resource type (e.g., operator pool + tool pool); Queue requests resources from both pools simultaneously

variations.name: With shift schedule
variations.change: Add Shift block to Resource Pool to vary resource availability over time (e.g., more operators during day shift than night shift)

shift_schedule.name: Shift Schedule
shift_schedule.description: A model where processing capacity changes over time according to a work shift schedule. Activity capacity is linked to a Shift block that defines working hours, breaks, and off-shift periods. Models any operation with scheduled capacity variation.
useWhen: Modeling a facility that operates on defined shifts (8-hour, 12-hour, or custom shift patterns)
Analyzing queue buildup during off-hours and the clearing time at shift start
Evaluating the impact of adding overtime or an additional shift on throughput and backlog
Modeling scheduled maintenance windows that take capacity offline periodically
Any system where server capacity is a time-varying schedule rather than a fixed number

blocks.name: Create
blocks.label: Arrivals
blocks.purpose: Generates items continuously (24/7 demand even when processing is offline during off-shift)

blocks.name: Queue
blocks.label: WorkQueue
blocks.purpose: Accumulates items during off-shift periods; drains when shift starts and capacity comes online

blocks.name: Activity
blocks.label: ShiftedServer
blocks.purpose: Processing activity whose capacity is controlled by the Shift block; capacity drops to zero off-shift

blocks.name: Shift
blocks.label: WorkSchedule
blocks.purpose: Defines the shift pattern: start times, end times, and capacity for each shift period; drives Activity capacity

blocks.name: Exit
blocks.label: Completions
blocks.purpose: Records completed items; throughput reflects the shift-constrained effective capacity

keyParameters.typical: [{startTime: 0, endTime: 8, capacity: 1}, {startTime: 8, endTime: 16, capacity: 3}, {startTime: 16, endTime: 24, capacity: 2}]
keyParameters.description: Defines capacity at each time period. Times are in simulation time units. Capacity=0 means off-shift. Multiple rows define multiple shift periods in one 24-hour cycle.
Processing time per item during active shift. Items in the Activity when a shift ends continue processing; items in Queue wait until next shift starts.

keyParameters.typical: Exponential, mean matching peak capacity utilization target
keyParameters.description: Arrival rate should be set so that total daily arrivals match expected daily throughput; if arrivals exceed daily capacity, backlog grows without bound.

keyParameters.typical: Integer values (1, 2, 3) representing number of simultaneous servers
keyParameters.description: Shift capacity values set the maximum concurrent items being processed by the linked Activity. Ensure Activity can handle the specified parallel capacity.

keyMetrics: Queue length at shift start (backlog carryover from previous off-period)
Time to clear overnight or off-shift backlog after shift starts
Effective throughput per shift period
Activity utilization during on-shift hours (not counting off-shift time)
Peak queue length during off-shift accumulation
commonMistakes: Not connecting the Shift block output to the Activity capacity connector — the Shift block has no effect on the Activity unless its value output connector is wired to the Activity's capacity input; without this connection the Activity runs at full capacity 24/7

Setting shift schedule times in wrong time units — if the simulation runs in minutes but shift start/end times are entered as hours (e.g., 8 instead of 480), the shift pattern will be wildly incorrect; always confirm simulation time units before entering schedule times

Forgetting that items already in the Activity when a shift ends continue processing to completion — the shift controls how many new items can start, not whether in-progress items finish; this is usually correct behavior but must be understood when analyzing shift boundary effects

Making total daily arrival volume exceed total daily capacity — if arrivals per day consistently exceed (capacity_per_shift * hours_per_shift / mean_service_time), the queue grows unboundedly across days; always verify that daily capacity exceeds daily demand

variations.name: Weekend/weekday schedule
variations.change: Extend Shift schedule to cover a 168-hour (7-day) cycle with different capacities on weekdays versus weekends

variations.name: Shift schedule with shared resources
variations.change: Add Shift block to Resource Pool instead of Activity to control the number of available shared resources by shift period

variations.name: Overtime trigger
variations.change: Add an Equation block that monitors queue length and sends a signal to increase Shift capacity above the normal level when backlog exceeds a threshold

tank_system.name: Tank Pipeline System
tank_system.description: A continuous flow model using Rate library blocks where material flows through a series of tanks connected by valves. Each tank stores fluid volume and each valve controls flow rate between tanks. Models liquid pipelines, chemical processes, or any continuous fluid system.
useWhen: Modeling continuous fluid or material flow (liquids, gases, bulk solids, chemicals)
Analyzing tank levels, overflow conditions, and flow rates in a pipeline
Simulating filling, draining, and steady-state flow in multi-tank systems
Modeling hydraulic systems, water distribution networks, or chemical processing
Any scenario where the quantity of interest is a continuously changing volume or rate

blocks.name: Tank
blocks.label: SourceTank
blocks.purpose: First tank in the pipeline; represents the source or supply reservoir with initial fluid level

blocks.name: Valve
blocks.label: Valve1
blocks.purpose: Controls flow rate from SourceTank to MidTank; rate can be fixed or controlled by a Value connector

blocks.name: Tank
blocks.label: MidTank
blocks.purpose: Intermediate storage tank; level rises or falls based on inflow from Valve1 versus outflow through Valve2

blocks.name: Valve
blocks.label: Valve2
blocks.purpose: Controls flow rate from MidTank to SinkTank; enables independent control of each pipeline segment

blocks.name: Tank
blocks.label: SinkTank
blocks.purpose: Terminal receiving tank; accumulates material flowing through the system

keyParameters.description: Tank capacity sets the maximum volume. Initial level determines starting conditions. When level hits capacity, tank overflows; when level hits 0, tank empties and downstream valves close automatically.

maxRate sets the maximum possible flow rate. goal sets the desired flow rate. Actual flow is min(goal, available flow from upstream, capacity in downstream). Connect a Value connector to goal for dynamic control.
Physical rate limits at the tank inlet and outlet. Set to 0 to allow unlimited flow rate (limited only by connected valves). Use to model pipe capacity constraints.

keyParameters.typical: Equation block output or Constant block
keyParameters.description: For dynamic valve control, connect an Equation or Constant Value.lbr block to the Valve goal connector. This enables level-based control, schedules, or feedback regulation.

keyMetrics: Tank level over time for each tank (fill/drain curves)
Flow rate through each valve (actual versus requested)
Time to empty or fill each tank under current flow rates
Overflow events (when any tank reaches capacity)
Steady-state flow rate through the system (when inflow equals outflow)
commonMistakes: Mixing Rate.lbr blocks with Item.lbr blocks in the same flow path — Tank and Valve are Rate library blocks and connect via flow connectors (not item connectors); attempting to connect a Create or Activity block directly to a Tank will fail; keep discrete item flow and continuous flow in separate model sections

Setting Valve maxRate higher than the Tank's maxOutputRate or the upstream Tank's available volume — the valve cannot push more flow than physically possible; actual flow is always limited by the most constrained element in the path

Not setting initial Tank levels — if all tanks start empty and the source tank has no inflow, the model starts with zero flow; set SourceTank initialLevel to a realistic starting value to avoid a trivially empty simulation

Forgetting that when a Tank empties, downstream valves automatically reduce to zero flow — this is correct physical behavior but can be mistaken for a model error; verify tank levels before investigating unexpected flow shutdowns

variations.name: With feedback level control
variations.change: Add Equation and Math blocks to compute a control signal based on MidTank level; feed control signal to Valve goal connector to maintain constant tank level

variations.name: Parallel tanks (blending)
variations.change: Use Merge block to combine flows from two parallel Valve+Tank branches into one output stream

variations.name: With flow divergence
variations.change: Add Diverge block after Valve1 to split flow between two downstream tanks at a configurable ratio

feedback_control.name: Feedback Control System
feedback_control.description: A continuous-time feedback control loop modeled with Value library blocks. A setpoint is compared against a measured output to compute an error signal, which drives a controller that adjusts the process input to minimize the error. Models PID controllers, thermostats, or any closed-loop regulation system.
useWhen: Modeling a closed-loop control system (thermostat, speed controller, inventory level controller)
Analyzing control system stability, overshoot, and settling time
Designing or tuning PID controller gains for a simulated process
Modeling any system where an output is continuously measured and used to adjust an input
Evaluating control system response to disturbances or setpoint changes

blocks.name: Constant
blocks.label: Setpoint
blocks.purpose: Provides the desired target value (setpoint) that the controller tries to achieve

blocks.name: Math
blocks.label: ErrorCalc
blocks.purpose: Computes the error signal as (Setpoint - MeasuredOutput); provides the feedback comparison

blocks.name: Equation
blocks.label: Controller
blocks.purpose: Implements the control law (proportional, integral, or PID); takes error as input and outputs control action

blocks.name: Holding Tank
blocks.label: Process
blocks.purpose: Integrates the control action over time to produce the process output (state variable); represents system dynamics

blocks.name: Equation
blocks.label: FeedbackSensor
blocks.purpose: Reads the current Holding Tank level (process output) and feeds it back to the ErrorCalc block to close the loop

keyParameters.description: The desired steady-state value the controller attempts to maintain. The controller drives error (Setpoint - Output) toward zero.

keyParameters.typical: Kp * e (proportional only) or Kp*e + Ki*integral(e) + Kd*derivative(e)
keyParameters.description: The control law. Proportional-only (Kp*e) is simplest but may have steady-state offset. Add integral term (Ki) to eliminate offset. Add derivative term (Kd) to reduce overshoot. Typical Kp=1-10, Ki=0.01-0.1, Kd=0-1.

The Holding Tank acts as an integrator: its level changes at the rate set by the inflow (control action). Initial level sets the starting condition. Capacity must be large enough to accommodate the full operating range.

The error signal. Positive error means output is below setpoint (controller should increase input). Negative error means output is above setpoint (controller should decrease input or flow is reversed).

keyMetrics: Settling time: time for Process output to reach within 5% of setpoint
Overshoot: peak deviation above setpoint as percentage of setpoint value
Steady-state error: remaining error after system settles (should be near zero with integral control)
Rise time: time for output to go from 10% to 90% of final value
Control effort: magnitude and variability of Controller output signal
commonMistakes: Forgetting to close the feedback loop by connecting the Holding Tank output back to the ErrorCalc block — without this connection the model is open-loop (no feedback); the output will drift without correction and the controller has no effect

Setting controller gain Kp too high — proportional gains that are too large cause oscillation or instability; start with a low gain (Kp=0.1) and increase gradually while observing the step response in the Holding Tank level

Using Equation block instead of Holding Tank for the process state — the process must be a dynamic element (integrator) to have meaningful control dynamics; an Equation block computes an algebraic (instantaneous) value, not a time-integrated state, which eliminates the dynamics the controller is designed to regulate

Not accounting for the sign of the feedback — if the Math block computes (Feedback - Setpoint) instead of (Setpoint - Feedback), the error signal is inverted and the controller drives the output in the wrong direction, causing instability; always verify error = setpoint minus measured output

variations.name: Proportional only (P controller)
variations.change: Set Controller equation to Kp * error; accept small steady-state offset in exchange for simplicity and stability

variations.name: PI controller (eliminate offset)
variations.change: Add a second Holding Tank to integrate the error signal; add Ki * integral_error term to Controller equation to eliminate steady-state error

variations.name: Tank level control
variations.change: Replace the Holding Tank Process block with actual Rate.lbr Tank+Valve blocks; feed Controller output to Valve goal connector to regulate tank level at setpoint
